Integrating Perinatal Mental Health into Maternal Care

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Angela Thomas, DrPH, MPH, MBA
3mo
  • Report this post

Maternal mental health is not a side issue in maternal health. It is a driver of outcomes. In our Safe Babies Safe Moms work, we saw clearly that you cannot improve maternal and infant outcomes without directly addressing perinatal mental health. Yet too often, systems are designed to screen without the infrastructure to treat. This is why we built an integrated perinatal mental health model within Safe Babies Safe Moms that does more than identify need. It responds to it. Our approach connects screening, clinical care, behavioral health, and social support into a coordinated system across pregnancy, birth, and postpartum. Because identifying depression, anxiety, or trauma is only the first step. What matters is what the system does next. In maternal health, gaps in follow-up are not just inefficiencies. They are risks. When mental health care is embedded into the structure of care delivery, not treated as an add-on, we begin to see what is possible. Better engagement Earlier intervention Improved outcomes for both mother and baby Maternal mental health is not separate from maternal safety. It is central to it. That is the work we are continuing to build through Safe Babies Safe Moms. #maternalhealth #mentalhealth #safebabiessafemoms

Neelam Prasad 3mo

You are absolutely right that maternal mental health is a drive of outcomes! As a premie mom of 2, I was not screened for any mental health issues despite my NICU experience and birth trauma. It is a broken system - no screening and no resources, or screening and still no resources. This is what my work currently focuses on now - advocacy for better outcomes with mental health screening and resources because if Mom is ok so will be baby.

This is so accurate! I believe this is where the occupational therapy role is so under utilised because we are trained to offer care & services to populations that experience occupational disruption. My goal is to firstly, produce the research backing the impact of occupational therapy in women’s health especially during the transitional periods of pregnancy and menopause tailoring it to the African continent. And secondly to break into this space of actually treating maternal mental health! I think as healthcare professionals we need to realise that the conventional way of previously being rigid and non-adaptive to the changes we are seeing in terms of what’s now causing disability, should change.

  • William G. Hudson
    3mo
    • Report this post

    I’m truly honored to have my story included in Someone Like Me, a powerful storytelling series created by Kay Matthews and the Shades of Blue Project. Kay’s leadership continues to set the standard for what compassionate, community‑rooted maternal mental health support looks like. What makes this work so meaningful is its honesty. Maternal mental health doesn’t begin and end with the birthing person, it touches partners, families, and the entire village walking alongside them. Sharing these stories helps reduce stigma, normalize real conversations, and remind people that support is a collective responsibility. This mission aligns deeply with the work I’m advancing every day: using technology to listen at scale, understand lived experience more clearly, and elevate the stories that shape better systems of care. When we combine community‑rooted storytelling with modern experience‑management tools, we create a more complete picture of what families need, and we build pathways that are more responsive, more human, and more equitable. That’s why I’m proud to support the SHARE Network, a maternal mental health resource hub designed to make support easier to find, easier to share, and easier to talk about. My story reflects the reality that partners and families need tools, education, and connection too. When we include the full village, outcomes improve and trust deepens. Stories change culture. Technology helps us hear more of them. And organizations like Shades of Blue ensure those stories lead to action. Grateful to Kay and her team for creating space for voices like mine, and for reimagining maternal mental health in a way that centers community, dignity, and truth. Kay Matthews Shades of Blue Project Qualtrics Mom Congress Maternal Mental Health Leadership Alliance (MMHLA) Center for Black Maternal Health and Reproductive Justice at Tufts University

      • Maternal Mental Health Leadership Alliance (MMHLA)

        17,453 followers

        2mo
        • Report this post

        Mental health conditions are the most common complications of pregnancy and childbirth, impacting at least 1 in 5 women during the perinatal period, with individuals from historically marginalized communities at even higher risk. Approximately 75% of those affected remain untreated. To help health care professionals learn more about these conditions, Maternal Mental Health Leadership Alliance and CVS Health have curated a database of perinatal mental health trainings. Our user-friendly, searchable database makes it easy to find a training based on your schedule, profession, and topic of interest. Learn more at https://hubs.la/Q046KWX80

        • Eunice Benu
          2mo
          • Report this post

          What happened to me is not rare. Across Nigeria, many mothers face disruptions to their breastfeeding journey due to: • Illness • Work demands • Early formula introduction • Lack of accurate guidance And when these challenges arise… the system is often silent. Hospitals focus on the baby’s immediate needs—but ongoing lactation support for mothers is limited. Mental health support? Almost non-existent. So mothers are left to figure it out alone. And the cost is high. Many experience: • Painful physical complications (engorgement, blocked ducts, infections) • Emotional distress, anxiety, and self-doubt • Exhaustion from trying to “fix” things without help Some even feel like they have failed. But the truth is this: 👉 This is not a personal failure. It is a system failure. Breastfeeding is not just about awareness. It is about support, structure, and access. #MaternalCare #HealthcareNigeria #BreastfeedingSupport #PublicHealth #WomenSupportingWomen

          So very true! Working with clients at risk or diagnosed with PND (Post Natal Depression) usually mums, every day, I see that her partner’s struggles become her 2nd biggest woe. Just this week I heard about her partner’s: - defensiveness and shut down - repressed emotional awareness - poor stress management - overwhelm - avoidance and zoning out - lack of assertiveness - inability to identify & express emotional needs - role conflict and lack of understanding the importance of theirrole - lack of assertiveness skills - immature differentiation from own parents - unhealed attachment wounds - isolation and feeling of loneliness Taking care of our men when they become dads is the easiest opportunity to help them grow, set their families on a healther path, can break society’s toxic cycles and might just improve whole communities’ wellbeing going forward.

          Dr Robinah Namulindwa

          GP/ Clinical Chartered Manager/ Maternal and Child Health Specialist / Public Health Advocate / President @ CLIPASA France 🇫🇷 and Suisse 🇨🇭/Author of 4 Influential books tackling maternal mortality/ PHD.

          2mo

          🧠🤰 The Silent Influence: How Men’s Mental Health Shapes Pregnancy and Baby Outcomes When we talk about maternal and newborn health, the spotlight is almost always on the mother—and rightly so. But there is a quieter, often overlooked factor that significantly shapes outcomes: men’s mental health. Behind every pregnancy is a partner, a father, a man navigating his own fears, pressures, and expectations. And when his mental health is strained, the ripple effects can reach both mother and baby. Here’s what we don’t talk about enough: 🔹 Emotional Support Matters A mentally well partner is more likely to provide consistent emotional support—reducing stress and anxiety for the mother. This directly contributes to healthier pregnancies. 🔹 Stress is Contagious When men experience untreated stress, depression, or anxiety, it can create tension at home. This environment can increase the risk of complications such as preterm birth and low birth weight. 🔹 Engagement in Care Men struggling mentally may withdraw from antenatal care involvement, decision-making, or financial preparation—impacting timely access to care. 🔹 Breaking Harmful Norms Many men are taught to “stay strong” and suppress emotions. But silence can be dangerous. Encouraging men to seek help is not weakness—it is responsibility. What must change? ✔️ Include men in maternal health conversations—not as bystanders, but as active participants ✔️ Normalize mental health support for fathers before, during, and after pregnancy ✔️ Design community programs that address family well-being, not just maternal health ✔️ Encourage open dialogue in homes, clinics, and communities Healthy families begin with healthy individuals—both women and men. If we are serious about reducing maternal and newborn mortality, we must widen our lens. Because sometimes, the most powerful influences are the ones we do not see. 💬 Let’s start the conversation: How can we better support men’s mental health in maternal care? #MaternalHealth #MensMentalHealth #NewbornHealth #FamilyHealth #PublicHealth #EndMaternalMortality #WHO

About Someone Like Me Someone Like Me centers community rooted storytelling that helps families feel seen and less alone. The series creates space for honesty, compassion, and the reminder that support is for the entire family system. How this connects to SHARE Network Nicole and Marcus’s story connects directly to the purpose of SHARE Network because maternal mental health impacts more than one person. SHARE Network is a maternal mental health resource hub designed to make supportive tools, education, and resources easier to find and easier to share across pregnancy and postpartum. Their story reinforces why support partners should be included, equipped, and empowered to show up effectively. Why this showcase matters Stories like Nicole and Marcus’s help shift the culture so families feel safe talking about what they are experiencing and reaching out earlier.           • Renée Crown Wellness Institute CU Boulder

            1,056 followers

            2mo
            • Report this post

            New and expectant moms with depression are often forced to choose between medication or nothing—assuming they have access to care at all. Behavioral tools that work exist. But therapy is rarely available in obstetric care. That leaves too many women navigating pregnancy without meaningful mental health support. But what if support came from someone who’s not only been there, but comes from the same community? Alma is a peer-delivered program that teaches Behavioral Activation skills — practical tools proven to reduce depression. [use white alma logo top left] We co-designed Alma with moms and community partners to make it culturally grounded and accessible. In a pilot randomized controlled trial (RCT), women receiving peer-delivered Alma showed promising reductions in depressive symptoms. The program was feasible, accessible, and built on trust. Turns out, community medicine is powerful. Caring for mothers is caring for everyone. Alma is a step toward expanding real-world mental health support in pregnancy. 🔗 Learn all about this incredible program here: https://lnkd.in/gBV7fVRZ
